title: Lance of Longinus and Arael Speed and orbit height
display_title: Lance of Longinus and Arael Speed and orbit height
wikitext:
[]: Lance of Longinus and Arael: Speed and orbit height             Quote Post by alfrsa » Tue Jun 28, 2011 10:46 pm Since I’m not able to construct or analyze theories like most of you (so well) do and I have been lurking for a while now, I thought it’d be fun to contribute with some facts. I had a bit of free time, so I set out to do something I meant to do for a while now: calculate the height of Arael’s orbit. To do so, I had to assume the following is true: - The speed of the lance is constant, before and after destroying Arael; since LnY has shown to be capable of propelling itself and regained speed after hitting Arael’s ATF, I took it that it would move towards the angel/moon as fast as it could. - The dialog scene between the impact with the angel and the shot of the Lance floating near the moon share the same time continuity (meaning the people on Earth witnessed the Lance approaching the angel and continue after destroying it at the same time as we, as one continuous scene). - The lance followed a straight line all the way to the moon, having suffered negligible influence from gravity and drag/friction. Given its (enormous) speed, I think it's reasonable to believe so. Also, since the mass, acceleration and initial velocity of the lance are unknown, the only thing I was able to take out of the anime was the mean velocity; had I had those, I think I could have applied the equations of motion. Note:I only have an average knowledge of physics, so to the pros around, please point out any mistakes/misassumptions I may have committed. So, on we go: The speed of the Lance v= ∆s/∆t I measured the time it took to the lance to reach Arael 3 times, using a chronometer, watching the launch at 25% speed. (The times are adjusted for normal playing speed). From Eva-00’s hand to the ATF, the average time is 7.05 seconds. From Arael to the moon, I figured (as did the OP in this thread) it was about 11 seconds. The time from Tokyo-3 to the moon was 11+7.05=18.05 s Mean distance from the Earth to the moon = 385000 Km ∆v Lance = 385000/18.05 = 21329.64 Km/s = 21329640 m/s The lance travels at 21329 Km/s, or 13253 mph ! Speed of light = 299792458 m/s v Lance / v Light = 7.11% The Lance moves at 7.11% the speed of light ! Distance between Eva-00 and Arael: Launch angle: SPOILER: Show

[]:             Quote Post by child of Lilith » Tue Jun 28, 2011 10:49 pm More proof of how awesome Rei is.

[]:             Quote Post by EvangelionFan » Tue Jun 28, 2011 10:54 pm Your math seems sound. Nice work. I wonder if we have enough info to calculate the force with which Unit-00 has to hurl the Lance in order for it to reach that velocity. Cause, y'know, that would be wonderful .

[]:             Quote Post by alfrsa » Tue Jun 28, 2011 11:09 pm I tried to reach the launch force; we're missing mass or acceleration. If we had one of those, we could figure it out... or so I think... Oh man, now I HAVE to find a way!

[]:             Quote Post by Fireand'chutes77 » Tue Jun 28, 2011 11:20 pm We were studying stuff like this last semester in my aerospace engineering class. I approve. :) You know, *this* is how they should teach physics in high school! A physics professor at college actually used Velociraptors and the Terminator on tests for sound propagation and electromotive forces, respectively. He was pretty awesome. (Sample test question: A raptor is running toward you at a constant [something] mph while you run away at a constant [something else] mph. At time t = 0, you hear its celebratory hunting scream. Alan Grant told you Velociraptors scream at [x] hertz. How long can you run until you are chased down and eaten?)

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Wed Jun 29, 2011 12:19 am You are a total nerd and need to get out more, but holy shit you're awesome.

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Wed Jun 29, 2011 12:59 am The only inconsistencies with what we see in both the Director's Cut and On-Air versions of 22 is that the trajectory is parabolic (thus not straight) and it doesn't actually hit the moon as fight after Arael is destroyed, it's just spinning around in space (meaning that it slowed down significantly). The problem with taking both of these into account is you'd need to either know the initial acceleration that the Lance was thrown with, because it's decelerating from that point on up to interacting with Arael, and/or you'd need to know the constant acceleration applied to (by) the Lance if the Lance was propelling itself. Eitherway, the Lance is going REALLY fast in order to reach Arael in that span of time.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Wed Jun 29, 2011 1:27 am If only mathematics questions in school exam are this inspring! Reminds me of how a bunch of fans working over time to solve the college level mathematics problem shown in Madoka anime....

[]:             Quote Post by alfrsa » Wed Jun 29, 2011 8:23 am @Fireand'chutes77: You're right! I remember a particular exercise on my 11th grade, in which we had to calculate the best path to escape a tiger that moved in a sine wave (...a bit tipsy, perhaps?)... That kind of physics is fun to learn! @AuraTwilight: Thanks, glad you liked it :) Anyway, I think there's a way to find the spear's launch energy; what if we used the radius of displaced clouds to find out the mass of water moved? To do so, we would need the average density of a rain cloud (since it's raining) and the thickness of the cloud layer (we could either use one of the frames from the launch or an average experimental value) and to measure the diameter of the cloud-free circle (we can measure it against Japan’s shoreline. I have to study for a final math exam and, as such, won't be able to look into this before monday, but what do you think?
